channel.title: S&P Septic & Excavating Inc. — Blog
channel.description: Educational articles about septic systems, maintenance tips, and excavation services for homeowners in Warren, Ohio and Trumbull County.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 S&P Septic and Excavating Inc.

item.title: How Often Should You Pump Your Septic Tank?
item.description: One of the most common questions homeowners ask: how often does my septic tank need to be pumped? The short answer is every 3 to 5 years, but it depends on household size, water usage, and tank capacity. Learn what affects pumping frequency and why regular pumping can prevent a $10,000+ drain field replacement.

item.title: 7 Warning Signs Your Septic System Is Failing
item.description: A failing septic system is more than inconvenient — it can cause serious health hazards and expensive damage. Learn the seven red flags every Trumbull County homeowner should know, from slow drains throughout the house to sewage backup.

item.title: Septic vs. City Sewer: Which Is Better for Ohio Homeowners?
item.description: If you're deciding between a property on septic or city sewer in Ohio, understand the key differences in costs, maintenance responsibilities, and long-term considerations for each option.

item.title: What NOT to Flush: A Homeowner's Guide to Septic Safety
item.description: Your septic system is not a trash can. Learn which items should never go down your drains — including the so-called "flushable" wipes that cause thousands of dollars in damage every year.

item.title: Excavation for Driveways: What Ohio Homeowners Need to Know
item.description: From grading to gravel base to final surface, proper driveway excavation requires the right equipment and expertise. Learn what Trumbull County homeowners should know before starting an excavation project.

item.title: Perk Testing & Soil Evaluation: Why It Matters for Your Septic System
item.description: Percolation testing is required before any new septic system installation in Ohio. Learn how perk testing works, why soil composition matters, and how S&P Septic handles the entire permitting process for new installations.
